---
title: "Values, Variables, and Types"
description: "Understand values, expressions, assignment, names, and type conversion together."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Understand values, expressions, assignment, names, and type conversion together.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Values, Variables, and Types** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Values and Expressions** — Recognize Python values and combine them with operators to produce new values.
- Section 2: **Variables and Assignment** — Use names to remember values and make programs readable.
- Section 3: **Types and Conversion** — Understand common types and convert between them deliberately.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
The picture below separates three ideas beginners often mix together: the code
line, the name created by assignment, and the value object Python remembers.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  line["Python line<br/>price = 10"] --> assign["assignment<br/>="]
  assign --> name["name<br/>price"]
  name --> object["value object<br/>10"]
  object --> type["type<br/>int"]
  textline["Python line<br/>price = '10'"] --> textname["name<br/>price"]
  textname --> textobject["value object<br/>'10'"]
  textobject --> texttype["type<br/>str"]
```

Use the diagram as a reading routine: find the line of code, identify the name,
then ask what value and type that name points to.
:::

## Section 1: Values and Expressions

Recognize Python values and combine them with operators to produce new values.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

An expression is like a calculator entry: Python evaluates it and gives one result back.
:::

### Mental model

A value is a piece of information; an expression is a question Python can answer with a
value.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
2 + 3
10 / 4
"Py" + "thon"
```

### Walkthrough

1. `2 + 3` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `10 / 4` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `"Py" + "thon"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Quotation marks change meaning. 42 is a number, but "42" is text.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "values-variables-types",
  "title": "Values, variables, and types checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "quoted-number-type",
      "prompt": "What is the type of the value \"42\"?",
      "options": [
        "int",
        "float",
        "str",
        "bool"
      ],
      "answer_index": 2,
      "explanation": "Quotation marks create text, so \"42\" is a string even though it contains digits."
    },
    {
      "id": "integer-type",
      "prompt": "What is the type of the value 42?",
      "options": [
        "str",
        "int",
        "bool",
        "list"
      ],
      "answer_index": 1,
      "explanation": "Whole numbers without quotation marks are integers."
    },
    {
      "id": "assignment-meaning",
      "prompt": "What does this line do: price = 10?",
      "options": [
        "It checks whether price already equals 10.",
        "It assigns the value 10 to the name price.",
        "It prints the value 10.",
        "It creates a text value named price."
      ],
      "answer_index": 1,
      "explanation": "In Python, a single equals sign assigns a value to a name. Use == when you want to compare two values."
    },
    {
      "id": "string-addition",
      "prompt": "Why does \"2\" + \"3\" produce \"23\" instead of 5?",
      "options": [
        "Python made a math mistake.",
        "The plus sign always creates text.",
        "Both values are strings, so + joins them together.",
        "Strings cannot use the plus sign."
      ],
      "answer_index": 2,
      "explanation": "When both operands are strings, + performs concatenation: it joins text together."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Variables and Assignment

Use names to remember values and make programs readable.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A variable is like a sticky note on an object. The note is not the object; it helps you
find the object again.
:::

### Mental model

Assignment attaches a name to a value so later code can reuse it.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
price = 12
quantity = 3
total = price * quantity
print(total)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `price = 12` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `quantity = 3` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `total = price * quantity` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(total)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
A single equals sign assigns. A double equals sign compares. Mixing them changes the
question you are asking Python.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-variables-and-assignment",
  "title": "Variables and Assignment checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Variables and Assignment?",
      "options": [
        "Assignment attaches a name to a value so later code can reuse it.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Assignment attaches a name to a value so later code can reuse it."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A variable is like a sticky note on an object. The note is not the object; it helps you find the object again."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Types and Conversion

Understand common types and convert between them deliberately.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A type is like a label on a box: flour, sugar, and salt may look similar, but recipes
treat them differently.
:::

### Mental model

Types tell Python what operations make sense for a value.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
age_text = "12"
age = int(age_text)
print(age + 1)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `age_text = "12"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `age = int(age_text)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(age + 1)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Python will not always guess the conversion you wanted. Convert input text explicitly
before doing arithmetic.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-types-and-conversion",
  "title": "Types and Conversion checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Types and Conversion?",
      "options": [
        "Types tell Python what operations make sense for a value.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Types tell Python what operations make sense for a value."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A type is like a label on a box: flour, sugar, and salt may look similar, but recipes treat them differently."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Values and Expressions**: A value is a piece of information; an expression is a question Python can answer with a
value.
- **Variables and Assignment**: Assignment attaches a name to a value so later code can reuse it.
- **Types and Conversion**: Types tell Python what operations make sense for a value.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Built-in types: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
